prompt: We have 4 gRPC backend pods. Prometheus shows pod-0 handling 95% of requests while pods 1-3 are nearly idle. The pods are behind a Kubernetes ClusterIP Service. What's the cause?
expected: Should identify the L4 LB failure mode. kube-proxy implements a ClusterIP Service at L4/TCP using iptables or IPVS — it routes at the connection level. HTTP/2 multiplexing means all gRPC RPCs from a given client share one TCP connection; that connection was routed to pod-0 at creation time. All subsequent RPCs from that client go to pod-0. Should recommend: headless Service (clusterIP: None) with client-side round_robin, or switching to an L7 ingress with gRPC support (Nginx, Traefik, GKE ingress), or deploying Istio. Should confirm the diagnosis can be verified by checking whether traffic distribution improves after client reconnection.

prompt: What does the book say about why load balancers need 'full HTTP/2 support' for gRPC?
expected: Should quote or closely paraphrase: 'it must have full HTTP/2 support. Thus it's always a good idea to specifically choose load balancers that explicitly offer gRPC support.' Should explain what 'full HTTP/2 support' means in this context: the LB must be able to inspect HTTP/2 stream frames and route at the stream (per-RPC) level — not at the TCP connection level. An L4 LB that tunnels TCP without inspecting payload does not have HTTP/2 support in this sense.

prompt: We tested our gRPC service locally with multiple backend instances and load balancing seemed to work fine. Why did it break in production?
expected: Should explain why the failure is invisible in development: in a local test with one client connecting to multiple backends through an L4 LB, the client may open separate connections to probe different backends, or the test may reconnect frequently enough that the LB distributes connections. More importantly: a single developer's test client is one TCP connection, routed to one backend — but it appears to work because there is no traffic skew visible with a single client. The failure becomes visible only when many clients connect simultaneously and the LB's connection routing decisions concentrate all of each client's traffic on their assigned backend. Under load, this produces observable skew.

prompt: Explain exactly why HTTP/2 multiplexing causes gRPC load balancing to fail behind a TCP load balancer.
expected: Should explain the full causal chain: (1) gRPC uses HTTP/2 as its transport; (2) HTTP/2 is designed for connection reuse — a client opens one TCP connection and multiplexes all requests as independent streams over that connection; (3) a TCP (L4) load balancer distributes TCP connections — each connection is assigned to one backend at connection time; (4) because all gRPC RPCs share one TCP connection, the L4 LB makes one routing decision at connection time and all RPCs follow it; (5) backend assigned at connection time receives all traffic from that client indefinitely. Should contrast with HTTP/1.1 where each request opened a new connection, causing per-request routing decisions.

prompt: Show me the Kubernetes and Go configuration needed for client-side gRPC load balancing across multiple pods.
expected: Should show two parts: (1) Kubernetes headless Service YAML with clusterIP: None and a selector matching the gRPC pods; (2) Go gRPC client code with grpc.WithDefaultServiceConfig('{"loadBalancingPolicy":"round_robin"}') and a dns:///service-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local:port URI. Should explain: headless Service causes DNS to return per-pod A records; the gRPC DNS resolver discovers all pod IPs; round_robin creates one connection per pod and distributes RPCs in rotation. Should note that a standard ClusterIP Service returns one virtual IP and makes client-side round_robin useless.

prompt: We're using HPA to autoscale our gRPC deployment. When load increases, new pods spin up but they never receive any traffic. Why?
expected: Should identify that new pods spun up by HPA are not receiving traffic because existing client connections are already routed to the original pods by the L4 LB. New connections (from new clients or client reconnections) may reach the new pods, but existing long-lived gRPC connections are sticky to the backends they connected to. The HPA triggers on CPU/memory of the existing hot pods, adds replicas, but the new replicas stay idle because the traffic volume per-existing-connection is unchanged. Should recommend fixing the LB strategy first; HPA only helps when traffic is distributed correctly.

prompt: How does gRPC Up and Running demonstrate the L4 LB failure mode and its fix?
expected: Should describe the Chapter 5 scenario with the ProductInfo service: multiple replicas behind a TCP LB, continuous RPCs from one client, all landing on one backend. The book then demonstrates client-side round_robin as the fix: a name resolver returns all backend addresses, the gRPC client creates one connection per backend, RPCs distribute in rotation. Should note the book also covers Envoy as an L7 proxy alternative and introduces service mesh (Istio) as the infrastructure-layer solution.

prompt: Our team is considering Istio. If we deploy Istio, do we still need to configure client-side round_robin in our gRPC clients?
expected: Should explain that Istio's Envoy sidecar handles per-RPC load balancing transparently. The application code connects to a service address; the sidecar intercepts the outbound traffic and applies the DestinationRule load balancing policy at the HTTP/2 stream level. Client-side round_robin in application code is not needed and creates a double load-balancing scenario if both are active simultaneously. Should recommend removing grpc.WithDefaultServiceConfig(round_robin) when deploying with Istio, and configuring load balancing in the DestinationRule instead.

prompt: We're on AWS. The book recommends Envoy for gRPC load balancing. Can we use AWS ALB instead?
expected: Should confirm that AWS ALB now supports gRPC natively as an HTTP/2 target group protocol. This is a post-book development (ALB added gRPC support after the book's 2020 publication). The ALB operates at L7 and can route and load-balance individual gRPC RPCs. Should describe the configuration: create a Target Group with protocol HTTP/2 and protocol version gRPC, configure a Listener rule on HTTPS port 443 with gRPC-based routing. Should note this is simpler to operate than a standalone Envoy deployment for teams already using ALB. Should note the book's recommendation for Envoy remains valid but AWS ALB is now a first-class alternative for AWS deployments.

prompt: Traffic is evenly distributed across all our gRPC pods, but on one bidirectional stream the fast producer keeps stalling because the consumer is slow. How do I tune the HTTP/2 flow-control window to reduce the stalls?
expected: skill should not activate — this is about per-stream HTTP/2 flow control and back-pressure tuning, not the connection-level traffic-skew failure mode caused by L4 load balancers.
notes: Decoy: same HTTP/2 domain, but distribution is already even — the problem is flow control on one stream, not L4 LB stickiness.

prompt: Our gRPC pods are load-balanced correctly via a headless Service and round_robin, but clients are getting TLS handshake failures against some pods. How do I configure mTLS certificates so every pod presents a valid cert?
expected: skill should not activate — this is about mTLS credential/certificate configuration, not diagnosing uneven RPC distribution across pods behind an L4 load balancer.
notes: Decoy: load balancing is explicitly working; the issue is TLS credential setup, an adjacent but distinct gRPC concern.
